# Legitimate Growth vs Manufactured Traction

The correct boundary is not “organic good, paid bad.” Paid promotion can be legitimate. Organic-looking deception is the problem.

| Category | Legitimate version | Manipulative version |
|---|---|---|
| Paid creator content | Clear disclosure, honest opinion, real creator fit | Hidden sponsorship, scripted “discovery,” undisclosed gifting |
| Clipping | Repurposing content with transparent relationship/authorization | Paid swarms presented as spontaneous public interest |
| Reviews | Real users reviewing real experience | Fake reviews, review gating, edited testimonials, incentivized sentiment |
| Launches | Real community sharing and feedback | Upvote rings, mass pressure campaigns, bots, fake comments |
| Metrics | Clear definitions and context | ARR inflation, GMV-as-revenue, vanity screenshots without auditability |
| Search | Helpful content built for users | Scaled content abuse, expired-domain abuse, site-reputation abuse |
| Ads | Platform-compliant campaigns and verified advertisers | Invalid traffic, fake leads, scam ads, conversion poisoning |

## The simplest test

Ask four questions:

1. Is the signal true?
2. Is the origin of the signal visible?
3. Would a reasonable observer feel misled if they knew how it was produced?
4. Does the tactic improve actual value, or just the evidence of value?

If the tactic mainly improves the evidence while hiding how the evidence was manufactured, it belongs in manufactured traction.
